---
title: "Solutions — Exercise Sheet 6: Multi-Electron Atoms"
subtitle: "EP4: Experimental Physics 4 — Atomic and Molecular Physics"
---

## Solution 1: The Helium Ground State

**(a)** $\hat{H} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}(\nabla_1^2 + \nabla_2^2) - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r_1} + \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r_{12}}$

The first two terms are kinetic energies, the middle terms are electron–nucleus attraction, and the last is electron–electron repulsion.

**(b)** $E_0 = 2 \times (-Z^2 \times 13.6) = 2 \times (-4 \times 13.6) = -108.8\;\text{eV}$

**(c)** Repulsion energy: $E_{\text{rep}} = E_{\text{exp}} - E_0 = -79.0 - (-108.8) = 29.8\;\text{eV}$

Fraction: $29.8/108.8 = 27.4\%$ — a very significant correction.

**(d)** $\frac{dE}{dZ_{\text{eff}}} = (-4Z_{\text{eff}} + 27/8) \times 13.6 = 0 \implies Z_{\text{eff}} = 27/16 = 1.6875$

$E = [-2(27/16)^2 + (27/8)(27/16)] \times 13.6 = -2(729/256) \times 13.6 + (729/128) \times 13.6$

$= 13.6 \times [-1458/256 + 729/128] = 13.6 \times [-5.695 + 5.695] = 13.6 \times (-729/256)$

$E = -13.6 \times 5.695/2 = -77.5\;\text{eV}$

This is within $2\%$ of the experimental value $-79.0\;\text{eV}$. The effective charge $Z_{\text{eff}} = 1.69 < 2$ reflects partial screening of the nucleus by the other electron.

## Solution 2: Exchange Interaction

**(a)** Symmetric: $\psi_+ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[\phi_{1s}(\mathbf{r}_1)\phi_{2s}(\mathbf{r}_2) + \phi_{1s}(\mathbf{r}_2)\phi_{2s}(\mathbf{r}_1)]$

Antisymmetric: $\psi_- = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[\phi_{1s}(\mathbf{r}_1)\phi_{2s}(\mathbf{r}_2) - \phi_{1s}(\mathbf{r}_2)\phi_{2s}(\mathbf{r}_1)]$

**(b)** Total wave function must be antisymmetric. Symmetric spatial $\psi_+$ pairs with antisymmetric spin (singlet, $S = 0$). Antisymmetric spatial $\psi_-$ pairs with symmetric spin (triplet, $S = 1$).

**(c)** The Coulomb integral: $J = \iint \frac{|\phi_{1s}(\mathbf{r}_1)|^2|\phi_{2s}(\mathbf{r}_2)|^2}{r_{12}}\,d^3r_1\,d^3r_2$

The exchange integral: $K = \iint \frac{\phi_{1s}^*(\mathbf{r}_1)\phi_{2s}^*(\mathbf{r}_2)\phi_{1s}(\mathbf{r}_2)\phi_{2s}(\mathbf{r}_1)}{r_{12}}\,d^3r_1\,d^3r_2$

Singlet energy: $E_S = E_0 + J + K$ (symmetric spatial → electrons closer on average)

Triplet energy: $E_T = E_0 + J - K$ (antisymmetric spatial → electrons farther apart)

**(d)** The **triplet** is lower because $K > 0$: the antisymmetric spatial function has a node when $\mathbf{r}_1 = \mathbf{r}_2$, so the electrons are on average farther apart, reducing the Coulomb repulsion. This is the origin of Hund's first rule.

## Solution 3: Electron Configurations

**(a)** Li: $1s^2\,2s^1$; Ne: $1s^2\,2s^2\,2p^6$; Na: $1s^2\,2s^2\,2p^6\,3s^1$; Ar: $1s^2\,2s^2\,2p^6\,3s^2\,3p^6$.

**(b)** Hund's rules in order: (1) maximize $S$ → $S = 1$ (triplet $^3P$); (2) maximize $L$ → $L = 1$; (3) shell less than half-full → $J = |L-S| = 0$. The triplet has lower energy due to exchange (Hund's first rule), and the specific $J = 0$ is lowest because the $2p$ shell is less than half-filled.

**(c)** Oxygen ($2p^4$): $S = 1$, $L = 1$, shell more than half-full → $J = L+S = 2$: **$^3P_2$**.

Titanium ($3d^2$): $S = 1$, $L = 3$, shell less than half-full → $J = |L-S| = 2$: **$^3F_2$**.

## Solution 4: Screening and Effective Nuclear Charge

**(a)** Orbitals with lower $\ell$ have more probability density near the nucleus ("penetration"), where they experience the full nuclear charge with less screening. So $3s$ penetrates more than $3p$, which penetrates more than $3d$, leading to $E_{3s} < E_{3p} < E_{3d}$.

**(b)** $5.14 = 13.6\,Z_{\text{eff}}^2/9 \implies Z_{\text{eff}}^2 = 5.14 \times 9/13.6 = 3.40 \implies Z_{\text{eff}} = 1.84$

**(c)** The bare nuclear charge is $Z = 11$, and sodium has 10 inner-shell electrons. If they perfectly screened the nucleus, $Z_{\text{eff}} = 1$. The result $Z_{\text{eff}} = 1.84 > 1$ shows that the $3s$ electron penetrates the inner shells and sees more than one unit of nuclear charge. This is physically reasonable.
